---
title: "Ch 3 — Coordinate Geometry"
subtitle: "Two number lines, four quadrants, every point named"
lesson-id: "cbse9.coordinate-geometry"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 3
ncert-chapter-title: "Coordinate Geometry"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse8.introduction-to-graphs", "t1.negative-numbers"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars", "cbse10.coordinate-geometry"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you read a value off a number line, including negatives?
   *(If no, revisit
   [t1.negative-numbers](../../tier-1-foundations/06-negative-numbers/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you draw a bar graph on a grid? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse8.introduction-to-graphs](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/13-introduction-to-graphs/index.qmd).)*
3. Do you know that $(-3) + 5 = 2$ and $(-3) \times 4 = -12$?

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Open a chessboard. Pick a piece, say a knight at **e4**. Name the square
two ways: by the letter–number system the chess world uses (`e4`), and by
the *row, column* pair counting from the bottom-left corner. Slide the
piece to **f6** — record both names again.

```{ojs}
viewof px = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "x"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof py = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 2, step: 1, label: "y"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const grid = 5;
  const size = 280;
  const c = size / 2;
  const u = (size - 20) / (2 * grid);
  let lines = "";
  for (let i = -grid; i <= grid; i++) {
    lines += `<line x1="${c + i*u}" y1="10" x2="${c + i*u}" y2="${size - 10}" stroke="#ddd"/>`;
    lines += `<line x1="10" y1="${c - i*u}" x2="${size - 10}" y2="${c - i*u}" stroke="#ddd"/>`;
  }
  const ax = `<line x1="10" y1="${c}" x2="${size - 10}" y2="${c}" stroke="#333"/>
              <line x1="${c}" y1="10" x2="${c}" y2="${size - 10}" stroke="#333"/>`;
  const dot = `<circle cx="${c + px*u}" cy="${c - py*u}" r="6" fill="#c33"/>
               <text x="${c + px*u + 8}" y="${c - py*u - 8}" font-size="13">(${px}, ${py})</text>`;
  return html`<svg width="${size}" height="${size}" style="border:1px solid #ccc">
    ${lines}${ax}${dot}
  </svg>`;
}
```

Drag both sliders. The red dot moves on a grid built from two number
lines crossed at right angles. Every position on the plane gets a name in
the form $(x, y)$.

## Wonder

1. The chess square `e4` and the pair $(5, 4)$ both name the same place,
   but in different ways. Could you invent a third way?
2. The point $(3, 2)$ sits to the upper right of the centre. Where does
   $(-3, 2)$ sit? $(-3, -2)$? $(3, -2)$?
3. Are $(2, 3)$ and $(3, 2)$ the same point?
4. The two number lines cross at one place. What is that place's name in
   the new system?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q3 is the trap. Q4 has a single, very simple answer that the *Name* beat
will state. Q2 is what we mean by "quadrants".
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1

A child stands at her front door. She walks $3$ paces east, then $4$
paces north. Mark her path on graph paper. Where is she relative to the
door?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Take east as the positive $x$ direction, north as the positive $y$
direction. Door is at the origin.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
She is at $(3, 4)$. East-of-door is the $x$-coordinate; north-of-door is
the $y$-coordinate. The order matters: $(3,4)$ is not the same place as
$(4,3)$.
</details>

### Problem 2

Plot the four points $A(2, 3)$, $B(-2, 3)$, $C(-2, -3)$, $D(2, -3)$ on
the same diagram. What kind of figure do they form?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A$ is upper-right; $B$ is upper-left; $C$ is lower-left; $D$ is
lower-right. Connect the dots in order.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A **rectangle**. Sides $AB$ and $CD$ are horizontal of length $4$; sides
$BC$ and $AD$ are vertical of length $6$. The four points sit symmetrically
about both axes.
</details>

### Problem 3

In which **quadrant** (upper-right, upper-left, lower-left, lower-right)
does each of these points lie?

$P(4, -1), \quad Q(-3, -2), \quad R(0, 5), \quad S(-1, 4).$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The sign of $x$ tells you left/right; the sign of $y$ tells you up/down.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P$: $x > 0, y < 0$ — **lower-right (Q-IV)**. $Q$: both negative —
**lower-left (Q-III)**. $R$: $x = 0$, so **on the $y$-axis** (no quadrant).
$S$: $x < 0, y > 0$ — **upper-left (Q-II)**.
</details>

### Problem 4

A point lies on the $x$-axis. What can you say about its $y$-coordinate?
A point lies on the $y$-axis. What can you say about its $x$-coordinate?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On $x$-axis: $y = 0$, so the point is $(x, 0)$. On $y$-axis: $x = 0$, so
the point is $(0, y)$. The origin $(0, 0)$ is on both axes.
</details>

### Problem 5

Plot $(2, 0)$ and $(2, 5)$. The segment between them is vertical. How
long is it? Now plot $(-1, 3)$ and $(4, 3)$ — how long is *that* segment?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For points sharing an $x$-coordinate, the distance is just the
difference of $y$-values. For points sharing a $y$-coordinate, the
distance is the difference of $x$-values.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Vertical segment: $|5 - 0| = 5$ units. Horizontal segment: $|4 - (-1)| =
5$ units. Both have length $5$.
</details>

You have just discovered: every point on the plane has a unique label
$(x, y)$; signs of the two coordinates split the plane into four
quadrants; points on the axes have one coordinate zero; horizontal and
vertical distances reduce to subtraction.

::: {.fun-fact}
**René Descartes** invented coordinate geometry in *La Géométrie* (1637),
the appendix to his *Discourse on Method*. The legend — possibly true —
is that he conceived the idea while lying ill in bed, watching a fly
crawl across the ceiling, and realising he could pin down the fly's
position at any instant by its perpendicular distances from two adjacent
walls. From that one image, the entire bridge between [algebra and
geometry](/glossary.qmd#coordinate-plane) was built. Newton and Leibniz
could not have invented calculus without it.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A point is just an *ordered pair*. Pinning a position needs two pieces
of information — across, and up — and the order is sacred: $(2, 3)$ and
$(3, 2)$ are different addresses on the same street. Geometry now has a
phone number for every point.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **Cartesian plane** is two perpendicular number lines (the $x$-axis,
horizontal; the $y$-axis, vertical) meeting at the **origin** $(0, 0)$.

Every point $P$ in the plane is named by its **coordinates** $(x, y)$:
- $x$ is the perpendicular distance from $P$ to the $y$-axis (positive
  right, negative left).
- $y$ is the perpendicular distance from $P$ to the $x$-axis (positive
  up, negative down).

The four **quadrants**, numbered counter-clockwise from the upper right:

| Quadrant | Sign of $x$ | Sign of $y$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| I (upper-right) | + | + |
| II (upper-left) | − | + |
| III (lower-left) | − | − |
| IV (lower-right) | + | − |

Points on the $x$-axis have $y = 0$; points on the $y$-axis have $x = 0$.

:::

A short verse:

> *Two lines that meet at right angles —*
> *Four quadrants, an origin, a name.*
> *Read $x$ first, then $y$;*
> *Order matters, lest two points share the same.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Plot $A(3, 4)$, $B(3, -2)$, $C(-1, -2)$. Name the figure $ABC$ might
> belong to and find the lengths of $AB$ and $BC$.

A competent solver thinks:

1. $A$ and $B$ share $x = 3$, so $AB$ is **vertical** with
   length $|4 - (-2)| = 6$.
2. $B$ and $C$ share $y = -2$, so $BC$ is **horizontal** with
   length $|3 - (-1)| = 4$.
3. The angle $\angle ABC$ is therefore $90^\circ$.
4. $ABC$ is a **right triangle**, right-angled at $B$.

**Answer:** Right triangle. $AB = 6$, $BC = 4$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Write the coordinates of the origin and of any point on the
positive $x$-axis at distance 5 from origin.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Origin $(0, 0)$; the point is $(5, 0)$.
</details>

**W2.** In which quadrant does $(-7, 2)$ lie?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x < 0, y > 0$ — **Quadrant II**.
</details>

**W3.** Plot $A(0, 3), B(-4, 0), C(0, -3), D(4, 0)$ and join them in
order. What shape is $ABCD$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A **rhombus** (in fact a square's cousin — a kite that is symmetric, so a
rhombus). Diagonals lie on the axes; lengths $6$ and $8$ on the two axes.
Each side has length $\sqrt{16 + 9} = 5$ — all equal, so it is a rhombus.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Three vertices of a rectangle are $A(1, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(5, 4)$.
Find the fourth vertex $D$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sketch. $D$ is across from $B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For a rectangle, $D$ shares its $x$ with $A$ and its $y$ with $C$:
$D = (1, 4)$.
</details>

**P2.** A point $P$ lies on the $y$-axis at a distance of $4$ units below
the origin. What are its coordinates?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On the $y$-axis means $x = 0$. Below origin means $y < 0$. So $P = (0, -4)$.
</details>

**P3.** Plot $(2, 3), (3, 2), (-2, 3), (-3, -2)$. Group them by
quadrant.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Q-I: $(2,3)$ and $(3,2)$. Q-II: $(-2, 3)$. Q-III: $(-3, -2)$. Note
$(2,3) \ne (3,2)$ — both are in Q-I but they are different points.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The vertices of a triangle are $A(0, 0)$, $B(6, 0)$, $C(0, 8)$.
Without using a distance formula, find the perimeter of the triangle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two sides lie on the axes — those are easy. For the third, you need the
hypotenuse of a right triangle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$AB$ has length $6$, $AC$ has length $8$. Use Pythagoras for $BC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = 6$ (along $x$-axis), $AC = 8$ (along $y$-axis). The angle at $A$ is
$90^\circ$. By Pythagoras $BC = \sqrt{36 + 64} = 10$. Perimeter
$= 6 + 8 + 10 = 24$.
</details>

**S2.** Plot the points $(1, 1), (1, 4), (4, 4), (4, 1)$. Now plot
$(2, 2), (2, 3), (3, 3), (3, 2)$. What relationship do you see between
the two figures, and how is it visible from the coordinates alone?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compare side lengths. Compare centres.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both are squares centred at $(2.5, 2.5)$. The first has side $3$; the
second has side $1$. The second is the first **scaled by 1/3 about the
common centre**. The coordinates make this scaling visible: each
coordinate of the inner square is the centre $2.5$ plus $\pm 0.5$, while
the outer is $\pm 1.5$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The point $(0, -7)$ lies on:
(a) Quadrant III (b) Quadrant IV (c) the $x$-axis (d) the $y$-axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = 0$ — **on the $y$-axis**. Answer: (d).
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Without plotting, in which quadrant or on
which axis do these points lie?
$A(2, -3), B(0, 5), C(-4, -1), D(7, 0)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A$: Q-IV. $B$: $y$-axis. $C$: Q-III. $D$: $x$-axis.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Plot the points $A(-2, 4), B(-2, -2),
C(4, -2)$ and find the area of triangle $ABC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Are $A, B, C$ aligned with the axes?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$AB$ is vertical. $BC$ is horizontal. So $\triangle ABC$ is right-angled
at $B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = |4 - (-2)| = 6$. $BC = |4 - (-2)| = 6$. The right angle is at $B$.
Area $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot AB \cdot BC = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 6 = 18$
square units.
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** Plot the points $P(1, 0), Q(4, 0), R(4, 3),
S(1, 3)$. Identify the shape and find its area and perimeter.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sides parallel to the axes; opposite sides should have equal lengths.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$PQ$: horizontal, length $3$. $QR$: vertical, length $3$. $RS$:
horizontal, length $3$. $SP$: vertical, length $3$. All four sides $= 3$;
all angles $90^\circ$. **Square.** Area $= 9$ sq.units. Perimeter $= 12$
units.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 3, Problem 3.2 (axis
classification, similar to 1-mark). **Previous-year pointer:** CBSE Class
9 sample paper "plot four points and identify shape" (similar to 4-mark).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Two perpendicular number lines name every [point](/glossary.qmd#point) on the plane.** That single act builds the bridge from algebra to geometry.
2. **Order matters.** $(x, y)$ is *across first, then up* — always. Swap them and you get a different point.
3. **Signs slice the plane into four quadrants**, plus two axes (one coordinate zero) and the origin (both zero).

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$(2, 3)$ and $(3, 2)$ are the same point."** No — order matters.
   The first coordinate is always $x$, the second is always $y$.
2. **"The $y$-axis is the horizontal line."** No — $x$ is horizontal, $y$
   is vertical. (The mnemonic: "$x$ is across, $y$ is high.")
3. **"$(0, 0)$ is in some quadrant."** No — the origin is on both axes
   and lies in *no* quadrant.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you sketch a labelled coordinate plane from memory and place a
   given point in the correct quadrant?
2. Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Can you read off horizontal and vertical distances between two points
   without thinking about a formula?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Linear Equations in Two Variables**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and *Apply*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse8.introduction-to-graphs`](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/13-introduction-to-graphs/index.qmd)
  — bar graphs, pictographs, simple line graphs.
- [`t1.negative-numbers`](../../tier-1-foundations/06-negative-numbers/index.qmd)
  — sign rules, the number line going both ways.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars`](../04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd)
  — every solution $(x, y)$ becomes a point.
- [`cbse10.coordinate-geometry`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)
  — distance formula, section formula, area of a triangle.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 3.1–3.2: the Cartesian plane, plotting
  points.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t2.coordinate-plane`](../../tier-2-algebra1/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd).
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every 2D game treats the screen as a coordinate
plane (with $y$ usually flipped, increasing downward). When a sprite
moves, the engine adds a velocity vector to its $(x, y)$. The chess board
in *Touch* is the simplest possible case — a discrete plane.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** A dataset of two-feature points (e.g. height vs weight)
lives in $\mathbb{R}^2$. A linear classifier draws a line. Generalised to
$\mathbb{R}^{784}$ (an MNIST digit), the same idea — points in a plane,
separated by a hyperplane — drives modern image classifiers.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (chessboard Touch), Boole (the
  Wonder questions surface the asymmetry $(2,3) \ne (3,2)$ before the
  text states it), Better Explained (the "Aha" is *one point, two
  numbers, in this order*).
:::
